heuristics.rule: Negotiate your starting salary as high as possible on OPT — it directly multiplies your H-1B lottery entries
heuristics.condition: When you are an international student on OPT negotiating a job offer with a company that may sponsor H-1B
heuristics.action: Push salary to reach DOL wage Level III or IV (OEWS-based), because the registration wage level determines lottery entries (3x or 4x vs. 1x), not just your take-home pay
heuristics.because: Under the FY2027 wage-based system, a Level IV salary gets 4 entries vs. 1 entry for Level I — this can shift selection probability from near-zero to near-certain if total registrations drop as projected

heuristics.rule: If your employer misses the 14-day March registration window, your H-1B opportunity for that fiscal year is gone — there is no late filing exception
heuristics.condition: When you are on OPT approaching the March H-1B registration period
heuristics.action: Confirm with your employer's HR or immigration counsel no later than February that they have your registration prepared and the $215 fee ready
heuristics.because: The registration window is only 14 days (March 4–19) and USCIS grants zero exceptions — missing it means waiting a full additional year

heuristics.rule: If not selected in the H-1B lottery, immediately evaluate cap-exempt employer options before giving up on US work authorization
heuristics.condition: When an H-1B registration comes back as 'Not Selected'
heuristics.action: Target universities, nonprofit research organizations, and government research organizations that are cap-exempt and can hire H-1B workers year-round without lottery
heuristics.because: Cap-exempt employers bypass the lottery entirely, allowing indefinite US employment authorization without competing in annual selection rounds

heuristics.rule: Do not let an employer register you at the salary's actual OEWS level if a higher level is technically eligible — insist on the highest level the salary qualifies for
heuristics.condition: When an employer is preparing your H-1B registration and your offered salary is at or near a level threshold
heuristics.action: Ask your employer's immigration attorney to register at the highest OEWS wage level the offered salary meets, regardless of job title classification
heuristics.because: OEWS registration level is determined by salary amount, not job title — registering one level higher can mean 1–2 additional lottery entries, multiplying selection probability

heuristics.rule: If you hold an advanced degree from a US institution, confirm your employer is filing under the master's cap exemption, not just the regular cap
heuristics.condition: When you have a US master's degree or higher and your employer is registering you for H-1B
heuristics.action: Verify with your employer's counsel that you are being entered in the 20,000 advanced degree exemption pool in addition to (or instead of) the regular 65,000 cap pool
heuristics.because: The master's cap provides an additional 20,000 visas annually — failure to use it wastes a significant statistical advantage you are entitled to

decision_factors.factor: Offered salary relative to DOL OEWS prevailing wage levels
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Under the wage-based lottery, salary determines the number of entries (1–4), which directly and dramatically changes selection probability
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Look up the DOL OEWS wage levels for your specific occupation and geographic area; determine which level (I–IV) your offered salary meets or exceeds

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Cap-exempt employers can file H-1B petitions at any time without lottery — this eliminates annual selection risk entirely
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check if the employer is a university, nonprofit research organization affiliated with a university, or government research organization

decision_factors.factor: Whether applicant holds a US master's degree or higher
decision_factors.why_it_matters: US advanced degree holders qualify for the additional 20,000 master's cap exemption pool, improving statistical odds
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Confirm degree conferral date and institution; degree must be from a US institution to qualify

decision_factors.factor: Total number of H-1B registrations projected for the filing year
decision_factors.why_it_matters: If total registrations drop significantly (50%+ as projected under wage-based deterrence), selection rates for higher-wage entries could reach 50–90%
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Monitor USCIS and immigration news in February–March for early registration volume signals and policy updates

decision_factors.factor: Availability of alternative visa pathways if lottery fails
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Students with L-1, O-1, TN, E-3, or J-1 eligibility have fallback options that preserve US work authorization without re-entering the lottery
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess nationality (TN for Canada/Mexico, E-3 for Australia), extraordinary ability evidence (O-1), and multinational employer structure (L-1)

decision_factors.factor: Employer's awareness of the new wage-based registration rules
decision_factors.why_it_matters: An employer who registers at the wrong OEWS level or misses the window due to process unfamiliarity can cost the employee their entire year's lottery chance
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask directly whether their immigration counsel has briefed them on the FY2027 wage-level entry system and OEWS vs. LCA distinction

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Accepting a lower starting salary on OPT to get a foot in the door at a desirable company
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: A lower salary may place you at OEWS Level I or II, reducing your H-1B lottery entries to 1–2 instead of 3–4, potentially cutting your selection probability by 75%
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Entry-level international students at brand-name companies known for underpaying early-career roles

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Relying on your employer to handle all H-1B registration logistics without independent verification
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: If the employer's HR misses the 14-day window, submits a duplicate, or registers at the wrong OEWS level, you lose a full year with no recourse
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students at companies with inexperienced immigration teams or high employee-to-HR ratios

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Working only for lottery-subject employers to maximize career options
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You subject yourself to annual lottery risk for potentially years, while cap-exempt employers would provide immediate, perpetual H-1B access without uncertainty
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students in academia-adjacent fields (research, data science, policy) who could realistically work at universities or nonprofits

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Targeting extremely large employers for H-1B sponsorship due to perceived stability
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Large employers with many H-1B registrations historically had more exposure to lottery stacking abuse; under the new beneficiary-centric rule, multiple employer submissions no longer help — only the salary level matters
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who have multiple companies willing to register them, believing this increases odds

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Not pursuing O-1 or other alternatives after a lottery loss because they seem out of reach
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: O-1 and L-1 have no numerical cap and no lottery — students who dismiss them early lose months of preparation time that could have secured continued status
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: High-achieving students in STEM, arts, or business with publication records, awards, or specialized expertise who underestimate their eligibility

failure_patterns.pattern: Employer submits duplicate H-1B registrations for the same beneficiary, resulting in automatic denial
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employer has multiple internal teams or staffing agencies each submitting independently, or attempts to game the system with multiple entries
failure_patterns.consequence: All registrations for that beneficiary are invalidated — the student gets zero entries in the lottery for that year
warning_signs: Student is working with a staffing agency AND a direct employer simultaneously
Employer has decentralized HR with multiple immigration vendors

failure_patterns.pattern: Missing the 14-day H-1B registration window due to delayed employer preparation
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employers assume the window is longer than it is, or HR teams deprioritize visa administration until it's too late
failure_patterns.consequence: Student loses an entire fiscal year of H-1B eligibility and must remain on OPT or leave the US
warning_signs: Employer has not mentioned H-1B registration by early February
HR is unclear on what documentation they need from the student

failure_patterns.pattern: Student registers at OEWS Level I because the job title is 'entry-level,' even though the salary qualifies for Level III
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employer or attorney conflates job title seniority with OEWS wage level; Level is determined by salary amount, not title
failure_patterns.consequence: Student receives 1 lottery entry instead of 3, dramatically reducing selection probability
warning_signs: Employer says 'you're entry-level so you'll be at Level I' without checking the actual OEWS salary thresholds
Immigration attorney doesn't ask for the specific offered salary before assigning wage level

failure_patterns.pattern: Student waits for H-1B lottery result before researching alternative visa options
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Optimism bias — students assume they will be selected and don't prepare contingencies until a denial forces them to act urgently
failure_patterns.consequence: By the time 'Not Selected' is confirmed (late March), there is insufficient time to build a strong O-1 case or find a cap-exempt employer before OPT expires
warning_signs: Student has never researched O-1 criteria or cap-exempt employers
Student's OPT expiration is within 6 months of expected lottery result

failure_patterns.pattern: Form I-129 filed at the wrong USCIS service center after selection
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employer files at a default location instead of the location specified in the selection notice
failure_patterns.consequence: Petition is rejected or significantly delayed, jeopardizing October 1 employment start date
warning_signs: Employer's immigration counsel does not reference the specific selection notice when preparing the filing
Employer reuses a prior-year filing template without updating the service center

diagnostic_questions.question: Does your offered salary meet DOL OEWS Level III or Level IV thresholds for your occupation and location?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether you will receive 3–4 lottery entries (strong odds) or only 1–2 (weak odds) under the wage-based selection system
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm employer will register at the highest eligible OEWS level, not just the job title's assumed level
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Evaluate whether salary negotiation is possible, or whether a cap-exempt employer is a better path

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your employer a cap-exempt organization (university, nonprofit research org, government research org)?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the H-1B lottery applies to you at all — cap-exempt employers can hire you year-round without lottery participation
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm the specific exemption category and ensure the petition cites it correctly; no lottery preparation needed
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Proceed with lottery strategy; assess salary level, registration timing, and alternative pathways

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you hold a master's degree or higher from a US institution?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether you qualify for the additional 20,000 advanced degree exemption pool on top of the regular 65,000 cap
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Ensure your employer's counsel registers you under the master's cap exemption and confirm degree conferral date is before the filing deadline
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: You compete only in the regular 65,000 cap; salary level becomes even more critical to maximize entries

diagnostic_questions.question: Has your employer confirmed they have immigration counsel briefed on the FY2027 wage-based OEWS registration rules?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the employer is likely to make a critical procedural error (wrong wage level, duplicate submission, missed window) that invalidates your registration
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Ask for the name of the firm and verify they have filed H-1B petitions in 2026 or later
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Escalate urgency with HR; offer to share official USCIS/DHS guidance; consider consulting your own immigration attorney independently

diagnostic_questions.question: If you are not selected in the H-1B lottery, do you have a viable alternative visa category (O-1, L-1, TN, E-3, J-1)?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether a lottery failure means leaving the US or whether you have a legitimate path to continued legal work authorization
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Begin preparing the alternative petition in parallel before lottery results so you can file immediately after a 'Not Selected' outcome
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Evaluate Canada immigration pathways (Express Entry, PGWP) or return-home career options as genuine contingency plans now, not after denial

diagnostic_questions.question: Are multiple employers or staffing agencies submitting H-1B registrations on your behalf?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Risk of duplicate submission — under the beneficiary-centric rule, multiple registrations for the same person from the same employer are invalidated
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Clarify whether the submissions are from the same or different legal employers; same-employer duplicates result in denial, cross-employer duplicates are now prohibited under the one-entry-per-beneficiary rule
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: No duplicate risk; focus on salary level optimization and registration timing

industry_realities.common_belief: The H-1B lottery is purely random, so salary and job level don't affect your chances
industry_realities.actual_reality: As of FY2027, the lottery is wage-weighted — a Level IV salary earns 4 entries vs. 1 for Level I, potentially increasing odds by 4x before accounting for reduced total registrations
industry_realities.implication: International students should treat salary negotiation as immigration strategy, not just financial planning — a $10K salary bump that crosses a wage level threshold could be worth more than years of career advancement

industry_realities.common_belief: Having multiple companies register you in the H-1B lottery increases your chances of selection
industry_realities.actual_reality: Under the beneficiary-centric rule effective FY2027, each unique individual gets only one entry regardless of how many employers submit — and duplicate submissions from the same employer result in denial
industry_realities.implication: Students should focus entirely on salary optimization at a single high-quality employer rather than seeking multiple registrations

industry_realities.common_belief: Cap-exempt employers (universities, nonprofits) are less desirable because they pay less
industry_realities.actual_reality: Cap-exempt employers provide H-1B access year-round with no lottery risk, no annual uncertainty, and the ability to transfer to a lottery-subject employer later while maintaining H-1B status
industry_realities.implication: For students in research, data, policy, or technical fields, cap-exempt employers are strategically superior immigration vehicles — even at slightly lower salaries — especially during high-uncertainty lottery years

industry_realities.common_belief: An 'entry-level' job title means you will be registered at OEWS Level I
industry_realities.actual_reality: OEWS wage level is determined by the offered salary relative to prevailing wage benchmarks for your occupation and location — not by job title. A high-paying 'junior' role can qualify for Level III or IV registration
industry_realities.implication: Students and employers should actively check OEWS thresholds and register at the highest level the salary supports, regardless of title — this is legal and explicitly permitted under the DHS rules

industry_realities.common_belief: The H-1B lottery is your only or primary path to long-term US work authorization as an international student
industry_realities.actual_reality: Multiple cap-exempt and non-cap pathways exist (O-1, L-1, TN, E-3, J-1, cap-exempt H-1B employers), and under the new wage system, better-positioned candidates may see selection rates approaching 50–90%
industry_realities.implication: Students should build a multi-track strategy: optimize for lottery while simultaneously preparing alternative petitions, rather than treating the lottery as the single make-or-break event

industry_realities.common_belief: The H-1B registration fee ($215) is refundable if not selected
industry_realities.actual_reality: The $215 registration fee is explicitly non-refundable regardless of selection outcome
industry_realities.implication: Employers bear a real per-head cost for each registration, which may influence whether smaller or less committed employers bother to register lower-priority candidates — students should confirm employer commitment early

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Negotiates salary based on cost of living, peer comparisons, and what feels fair — treats immigration implications as separate
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats every salary dollar above an OEWS wage level threshold as an immigration asset — negotiates specifically to reach Level III or IV to maximize lottery entries
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The difference between Level II and Level III registration can mean the difference between 2 and 3 lottery entries — a 50% increase in selection probability

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes selection is likely, waits for March results, and then scrambles to find alternatives if not selected
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Prepares a parallel alternative visa strategy (O-1, cap-exempt employer, Canada Express Entry) before March results arrive so they can act immediately on any outcome
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: OPT windows are short — losing 3–4 months to post-denial panic can mean the difference between maintaining status and having to leave the US

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Evaluating employer suitability for immigration purposes
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Evaluates employers on salary, culture, and career growth — asks HR 'do you sponsor H-1B?' and accepts 'yes' as sufficient
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Asks specifically: 'Which immigration counsel do you use?', 'At what OEWS level will you register my salary?', 'Have you filed under the new FY2027 wage-based rules before?', and verifies cap-exempt status independently
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: An employer who answers 'yes' to sponsorship but lacks experienced immigration counsel can still cost the student a full lottery year through procedural errors

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Dismisses universities and nonprofits as stepping stones or fallbacks with lower pay and lower prestige
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Recognizes cap-exempt employers as strategic immigration anchors — uses them to secure H-1B status without lottery risk, then transfers to desired industry employers later from a position of strength
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Students who secure cap-exempt H-1B status can transfer to any H-1B employer at any time without re-entering the lottery, giving them full career flexibility

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Tries to maximize lottery chances by having multiple employers or staffing agencies submit registrations simultaneously
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Knows the beneficiary-centric rule means multiple registrations provide no benefit and same-employer duplicates cause denial — focuses on optimizing a single, high-quality registration
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Attempting to game the system through multiple registrations now results in worse outcomes (denial) rather than better odds
